one_line_takeaway: A compact guide that unifies probabilistic robustness (the statistical proportion of adversarial examples under stochastic perturbation, framed for reliability/safety rather than worst-case security) and proposes -- but does not implement or test -- an AT-PR min-max training reformulation targeting the 'widest' all-AE region instead of the 'tallest' loss peak.
key_finding: The paper argues that PR, being a statistical/proportion-based measure, is more practical than worst-case AR for real-world safety-critical deployment, yet the field has a structural gap: while PR EVALUATION is relatively well developed (15 estimators tabulated), PR ENHANCEMENT is not -- only three prior works ([45,39,50]) explicitly improve PR and they yield 'quite limited improvement in AR'. There is no dedicated adversarial-training scheme for PR, which the paper's proposed AT-PR min-max reformulation (find the widest all-AE region, not the tallest-loss point) is meant to fill. The author also stresses that model-level PR alone is not safety: safety is a system property requiring reliability modelling, operational profiles, redundancy and uncertainty propagation to lift PR evidence to system-level claims.
core_contribution: A single-author survey/position book chapter (Springer, 2025) that consolidates the field of probabilistic robustness (PR) in deep learning. Its OWN contributions (distinct from the large body of cited work it reviews) are: (1) a unified presentation of PR's formal definitions and metrics -- local PR (Def. 1), global probabilistic Lipschitzness (Def. 2), and total statistical robustness / 'reliability' (Def. 3), plus tail-sensitive variants (rho-essential-supremum, CVaR, EVaR); (2) a NEW reformulated 'min-max' optimisation framework, termed AT-PR (Def. 4, Eq. 4), positioned as a theoretical foundation for adversarial training aimed specifically at improving PR rather than worst-case adversarial robustness (AR); (3) a systems-engineering discussion (Sec. 5) of how to translate model-level PR evidence into system-level safety/reliability claims; and (4) an agenda of open problems (Sec. 6). Note: the estimators catalogued in Table 1 are OTHER authors' prior work being surveyed, not this paper's results.
threat_model: The paper works within the adversarial-example (AE) setting -- inputs perturbed within an Lp norm-ball of radius gamma around x that flip the model's prediction -- so it sits squarely in the AI-security/robustness literature. However, the author deliberately DISTINGUISHES two regimes and does not adopt a worst-case attacker model: adversarial robustness (AR) covers 'worst-case and deterministic' scenarios that the paper explicitly ties to 'malicious contexts and security studies' and system-level SECURITY; probabilistic robustness (PR), the paper's subject, instead quantifies the PROBABILITY/proportion of AEs when inputs are perturbed STOCHASTICALLY according to a specified distribution within the norm-ball, and is framed as a reliability/SAFETY property for benign real-world operation ('worst-case evaluations are often unnecessary... in benign environments'). So the relevant 'threat' is not a strategic adversary but the statistical risk of encountering an AE under naturally-distributed perturbations; PR is deemed acceptable if AE risk stays below a threshold rather than being exactly zero.
limitations: Self-described as a 'concise' overview, not exhaustive (AR is explicitly out of scope; readers referred to other surveys). The headline AT-PR contribution is purely a formulation: the paper provides no algorithm, no proof of tractability, and no experiments -- it concedes the min-max is intractable analytically for non-convex DL models and that 'novel and efficient numerical algorithms... will be an important research direction.' No PR benchmark exists (acknowledged as an open problem), so the surveyed estimators are not compared head-to-head. PR research is heavily concentrated on image classification (per Table 1), with only single studies each on XAI ([22]) and text-to-image generation ([52]); extension to generative/multi-modal AI is future work. System-level integration (Sec. 5) is posed as open questions with only 'initial attempts,' lacking end-to-end case studies.

quantitative_results: Survey scope (meta, not experimental): 15 prior papers on PR-related metrics catalogued in Table 1, organised chronologically since 2019 (includes journal extensions of 2 conference papers).
Only 3 prior works ([45], [39], [50]) explicitly aim to improve (not merely evaluate) PR, and per the paper they give 'quite limited improvement in AR'.

Exactly 1 study each extends PR to XAI/interpretability ([22], SAFARI, ICCV'23) and to text-to-image generative AI ([52], ProTiP, ECCV'24); the rest of Table 1 targets image classification (a couple target regression/semantic perturbations).
Four canonical robustness problem formulations enumerated (binary existence, max safe radius, max prediction loss, statistical-inference/proportion), with PR corresponding to the fourth.
